title: Notifiable livestock disease reporting: what to do and when
subtitle: Your legal obligations when you suspect a listed disease.
excerpt: A smallholder who suspects a notifiable disease must contact their vet or APHA immediately. Delay is not just a welfare failure, it is a legal offence. Knowing which diseases are notifiable, what the early signs look like, and what happens after you report means you can act quickly if the situation arises.
sourceNotes: DEFRA, notifiable animal diseases list, gov.uk. APHA Animal Disease Reporting, apha.gov.uk. UK Animal Health Act 1981 and associated orders.

glossaryTerms.definition: A disease that keepers are legally required to report to the Animal and Plant Health Agency (APHA) if they reasonably suspect it in their animals. The duty to report exists even before a vet confirms the diagnosis.

content.text: Under the Animal Health Act 1981, any keeper who suspects a

in their animals must report at once to their vet or directly to APHA. The duty kicks in on reasonable suspicion, not confirmed diagnosis. A vet who sees the animal may also have their own duty to report.
The main notifiable diseases affecting small livestock

attrs.heading: Key notifiable diseases for UK smallholders

content.text: Foot and Mouth Disease (FMD): blisters on the tongue, lips, feet, and teats in cattle, sheep, goats, and pigs. Animals lame with mouth lesions. Newcastle Disease: sudden death or severe neurological signs in poultry; birds circling, twisting their necks. Avian Influenza (bird flu): sudden deaths in poultry flocks, respiratory distress, severe production loss. Swine Vesicular Disease: blisters on pigs' snouts and feet, similar appearance to FMD. African Swine Fever: severe haemorrhagic disease in pigs with sudden death. Scrapie: progressive neurological deterioration in sheep and goats (see separate tutorial). Anthrax: sudden death without premonitory signs; post-mortem haemorrhage from natural openings, do not open a carcass suspected of anthrax.

Call your vet immediately if you see signs consistent with any listed disease. Your vet will advise on whether to self-isolate the animals while APHA is notified. APHA's disease report line is 03000 200 301; you can report directly without going through a vet if you believe the risk is serious and immediate.

Once a notifiable disease is suspected or confirmed, movement of all susceptible livestock off the holding is prohibited until APHA lifts the restriction. This includes any market or show bookings. Do not move animals off the holding while waiting for an APHA response, this can spread the disease and transforms a welfare incident into a criminal offence.

Isolate affected or suspect animals from the rest of the herd or flock immediately. Do not allow visitors onto the livestock area. Change and disinfect footwear when moving between pens. The APHA officer will give specific biosecurity instructions on arrival; follow them precisely.
